enonce.texte: Read each sentence carefully. Identify the relative clause and decide if it is defining (essential to the meaning of the sentence) or non-defining (adds extra information, set off by commas). Then, choose the correct relative pronoun.

questions.texte: My brother ______ lives in Douala is a doctor. (I have only one brother)

sousQuestions.texte: What type of relative clause is used in question 1? Justify your answer.
sousQuestions.reponse: Non-defining. Because the speaker has only one brother, the information 'lives in Douala' is not essential to identify him. It is extra information, so commas are needed, and 'who' is used.

questions.texte: The student ______ won the scholarship is from Garoua.

sousQuestions.texte: Is this a defining or non-defining relative clause? Why?
sousQuestions.reponse: Defining. Because the clause 'won the scholarship' is essential to identify which student is being talked about. Without it, the sentence is incomplete. No commas are used.

questions.texte: Yaoundé, ______ is the political capital of Cameroon, has many hills.

sousQuestions.texte: Explain the use of commas in this sentence.
sousQuestions.reponse: The commas indicate that the clause 'which is the political capital of Cameroon' is non-defining. It provides additional information about Yaoundé, but the main sentence 'Yaoundé has many hills' is complete and clear without it.

questions.texte: The books ______ are on the table belong to the teacher.
My best friend, ______ father is the village chief, just got married.

sousQuestions.texte: Why can't 'that' replace 'whose' in this sentence?
sousQuestions.reponse: Because 'whose' indicates possession (the father of my best friend). 'That' cannot show possession. Also, the commas show it's a non-defining clause, and 'that' is generally not used in non-defining clauses.

intitule: Applying Relative Clauses in Real-life Contexts (Compétences)

enonce.texte: Read the following short texts about life in Cameroon. Combine the sentences using the correct relative pronoun (who, which, that, whose) and proper punctuation (commas for non-defining clauses).

questions.texte: Combine: The village has a new primary school. The school was built by a local NGO.
questions.reponse: The village has a new primary school, which was built by a local NGO.

sousQuestions.texte: Is your new sentence defining or non-defining? Explain.
sousQuestions.reponse: Non-defining. The village has only one new primary school (implied), so the information about the NGO is extra. The commas are essential.

questions.texte: Combine: The students participate in the 'Operation Net Cleanup'. Their commitment is exemplary.
questions.reponse: The students, whose commitment is exemplary, participate in the 'Operation Net Cleanup'.

sousQuestions.reponse: The students, the commitment of whom is exemplary, participate in the 'Operation Net Cleanup'.

questions.texte: Combine: The government launched a new campaign against malaria. The campaign aims to distribute free mosquito nets.
questions.reponse: The government launched a new campaign against malaria, which aims to distribute free mosquito nets.

sousQuestions.texte: If we remove the commas, how does the meaning change?
sousQuestions.reponse: If we remove the commas, it becomes a defining clause: 'The government launched a new campaign against malaria that aims to distribute free mosquito nets.' This would imply there are other campaigns against malaria that have different aims, making the clause essential to specify which campaign. The original (with commas) adds extra info.

questions.texte: Combine: Mr. Tata is the new discipline master. He is very strict but fair.
questions.reponse: Mr. Tata, who is very strict but fair, is the new discipline master.

questions.texte: Combine: The market woman sells 'poisson braisé'. Her grilled fish is the best in town.
questions.reponse: The market woman whose grilled fish is the best in town sells 'poisson braisé'.

sousQuestions.texte: Change the sentence to make the relative clause non-defining. What change in meaning occurs?
sousQuestions.reponse: New sentence: 'The market woman, whose grilled fish is the best in town, sells poisson braisé.' (Adding commas). Meaning change: The first sentence (defining) implies there are many market women, and I am specifying the one with the best fish. The second sentence (non-defining) implies I am talking about one specific market woman (perhaps already known) and adding that her fish is the best as extra information.

enonce.texte: Punctuate the following sentences correctly by adding or removing commas. Then, explain how the punctuation changes the meaning of the sentence.

questions.texte: The students who passed the exam will go on a field trip.
questions.reponse: The students who passed the exam will go on a field trip. (No commas - defining clause: Only the students that passed will go; others will not.)

sousQuestions.texte: Add commas to the sentence. How does the meaning change?
sousQuestions.reponse: The students, who passed the exam, will go on a field trip. (With commas - non-defining clause: All the students passed the exam, and all of them will go on the field trip. The clause just gives extra information.)

questions.texte: My uncle who lives in Bafoussam is a traditional healer.
questions.reponse: My uncle who lives in Bafoussam is a traditional healer. (No commas - defining clause: I have more than one uncle; the one in Bafoussam is the traditional healer.)

sousQuestions.texte: Add commas to the sentence. How does the meaning change?
sousQuestions.reponse: My uncle, who lives in Bafoussam, is a traditional healer. (With commas - non-defining clause: I have only one uncle. He happens to live in Bafoussam, and he is a traditional healer. The place is extra information.)
